summary: DeVault documents the process of porting Doom to Helios, his new microkernel operating system, as a real-world test of the kernel's design and capabilities. The port required four key features: a C programming environment, dynamic memory allocation, framebuffer access, and keyboard input. He built a PS/2 keyboard driver using Helios's notification-based IRQ handling, created a cross-compilation toolchain, and hacked together a minimal C library by pulling from musl libc. The resulting port is deliberately rough—with a broken sleep implementation, no save support, and crashes when picking up the shotgun—but successfully demonstrates the kernel's maturity and provides feedback on API design.
key_points: Helios implements IRQs similarly to seL4 using notification objects as IPC primitives, keeping the keyboard driver entirely in userspace as befits a microkernel
The C library was assembled by pulling large chunks of musl libc and writing shims to fake a Unix environment, acknowledging this as throwaway work
Helios's memory model requires userspace to allocate its own page tables, with a retry-based protocol when intermediate tables are missing
The port deliberately cuts corners (malloc that never frees, fake sleep timing) to prove kernel capabilities rather than build production software
Doom serves as an effective litmus test for OS kernel maturity because it requires just enough features to be meaningful without demanding a full POSIX environment
money_quotes: You never know if you have a good design until you try to use it for real.
I was able to write a very stupid malloc, which rounds all allocations up to 4096 and never frees them. Hey! It works, okay?
The result of all of this hacking and slashing is quite a mess, and none of this is likely to be useful in the long term.
So instead of trampling on his feet, I just yielded the thread and pretend it took 5ms to do so, hoping for the best. It does not work!
This port plays at wildly different speeds depending on the performance of the hardware you run it on.

It crashes when you try to pick up the shotgun. But it does its job: it demonstrates the maturity of the kernel's features thus far and provides good feedback on the API design and real-world utility.
key_insight: Porting a real program like Doom to a new OS kernel is a pragmatic way to validate design decisions and expose gaps, even when the port itself is deliberately rough.
